dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 responding for a reported seizure at the residential address in [CITY].
Hi, I'm EMT Mark, and this is my partner, Paramedic Dana. What happened today?
He had a seizure a few minutes ago. It was full body and lasted around 10 seconds.
Do you know who you are or where you are?
His eyes are open and reactive to stimuli. PERRL noted.
He's been speaking but is having trouble forming whole sentences.
He's moving all extremities but seems slightly disoriented.
Airway is patent and clear. He is breathing independently, no signs of obstruction.
His chest is rising and falling normally with equal bilateral breath sounds.
His pulse is elevated.skin tone appears normal, no cyanosis.
He was vomiting and laid face-down on the grass after it ended. He's been confused since.
Are you aware of where you are and why 911 was called?
I was standing with my daughter when I got a headache and became dizzy.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate this headache?
Probably a 4 or 5. It came on quickly.
There's a minor abrasion and bruising on the left side of his forehead.
His skin is intact with normal tone and temperature, no abnormal turgor.
Pupils are equal and reactive. No signs of Battle signs or epistaxis.
No jugular venous distention observed.
His mouth has a small cut on the tongue, no active bleeding observed.
Lung sounds are clear and equal on both sides.
No abdominal pain or distension noted on palpation.
Patient shows signs of urine incontinence, denies any pain in the lower abdomen.
Pelvis is stable. No pain or deformity detected upon movement or palpation.
Pulse is 130, regular.
Respirations are 20 and regular.
Blood pressure is 114 over 46.
We're going to check his blood sugar quickly.
Oxygen saturation is 70.
On the EKG monitor, it's showing sinus tachycardia.
Patient will require IV access. Dana, can you start an IV right now?
20g catheter in the right AC vein with normal saline at KVO.
Also, we've applied a 4-lead ECG monitor for continuous cardiac support.
Per our findings and protocol, this is a standard medical - seizure (protocol 3-12).
Patient is now more alert, responding better to questions. Alert and oriented x4.
Blood pressure now reads 155 over 66. Pulse is 117 and SpO₂ is back to 96. Respirations are 18 and regular.
IV is running smoothly, and EKG is monitoring properly with stable rhythm. Capnography is in place for respiratory tracking.
We'll be lifting you onto the stretcher. Please try to stay as still as possible.
He is now secured in a semi-Fowler's position with all safety straps fastened.
Hospital has been contacted, and [COMPANY_NAME] is our destination.
A detailed report has been provided via radio to the receiving hospital's ED.
Patient is rolled into the ED on stretchers, and condition reports were given on arrival.
A medication and protocol summary was handed off, and a nurse has received report and signed.
Normal saline IV remains running at KVO and capnography still connected for respiration tracking.
He was more alert and focused since he was moved into the ambulance.
SpO₂ per last was at 96 and no falling trend shown in initial post-ambulance updates.
His blood sugar is normal, around 102.
The daughter was also seated and buckled safely, and addressed in transport guidelines.
Communication with ED via radio was clear and perfectly flowed the procedure transfer.
Do you have any medications you take daily besides Depakote?
No. That is all for medications. I also don't have any known allergies.
Last time you ate before the seizure, was that breakfast or earlier?
Yep, I had eggs and toast about 45 minutes before it happened.
Tongue has a small cut but no bleeding on surface inspection.
He has no Battle signs or epistaxis.
No signs of blood or pain in the genital or urinary area that I saw.
Breathing is still normal, rhythm and depth have returned to baseline.
Pelvis is quiet, no pain on movement.
Second check shows low glucose.
Breathing remains at 18 and regular.
Communication with ED via radio was clear and perfectly flowed the procedure transfer.
All data confirms routine protocol observations per seizure patient guidelines.
Normal saline IV remains running slowly and in line with KVO instructions.
Communication with ED via radio was clear and perfectly flowed the procedure transfer.
Did you have any strange sensations, smells, or tastes before the seizure?
Nope, just a headache and dizziness. I didn't lose consciousness.
No abdominal pain even with deep palpation to sides.
Breathing remains at 18 and regular.
Reconfirmed protocol continues to guide safe and appropriate patient outcome.
Latest measurement includes SpO₂ at 96 and BP at 155 over 66.
All patient concerns were referenced in handoff and medical response was precisely prepared on stations.
His EKG is repeating sinus tachycardia but no changes denoting rhythm noteworthy.
Protocol has no remaining steps, the emergency is now transported and out of our reign.
He was successfully unloaded, bedded down, and team amenities were compliments accepted.
You have a known history of seizure disorder, right?
Yeah, diagnosis is epilepsy. Seizures have always been brief if not premeditated.
He appeared alert and oriented x4, in contrast to the initial post seizure confusion.
No pain reported at this time. Pain score is zero.
IV catheter still in site without infiltration concerns, fluids stay monitored for stability. Initial GCS was 14, later improved to 15.
All patient concerns were referenced in handoff and medical response was precisely prepared on stations.
All patient concerns were referenced in handoff and medical response was precisely prepared on stations.
SpO₂ per last was at 96 and no falling trend shown in initial post-ambulance updates. The patient reports no ongoing pain.
Final reviewed and no noted deviations, all events fall within medical protocol acceptance.
Hospital monitoring is now under command, and our ePCR is logged in system pending.
